phase_metadata.description:
Mastering the storage and manipulation of multiple
data elements using fixed-size Arrays, multidimensional structures, and the
flexible ArrayList.

learning_objectives: Declare, initialize, and access 1D and 2D arrays

Understand how arrays are
stored in Heap memory

Utilize the java.util.Arrays utility class for sorting
and searching

Identify the differences between fixed-size Arrays and dynamic
ArrayLists
prerequisites:
Knowledge
of loops, especially the 'for' and 'enhanced-for' loops (Phase 3)
introduction:
In programming, we often need to store a list of related items,
like high scores in a game or names in a guest list. Instead of creating 100
separate variables (score1, score2...), Java provides 'Arrays'. An array is a
container object that holds a fixed number of values of a single type. Think of
it like a numbered shelf where every slot is the same size.
concept_explanation:
An array in Java is an object. When you create an array,
the JVM allocates a continuous block of memory in the Heap. Every element in the
array is accessed by an 'index', which starts at 0. Because the memory is
continuous, accessing any element is incredibly fast. However, the trade-off is
that once an array is created, its size cannot be changed.

breakdown_of_syntax.explanation:
The type
of data followed by square brackets indicates an array variable.

The keyword used to allocate memory on the
Heap for the array object.

An
integer specifying exactly how many elements the array will hold.

The Varargs symbol, allowing a method to
accept zero or many arguments of the specified type.

rules_and_key_points: Arrays are 0-indexed: the first element is at [0].
The length of an array is fixed after initialization.

Accessing an index
outside the range (0 to length-1) throws ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.
Arrays can store both primitives and objects.

The 'length' property (not a
method) gives the size of the array.

2D arrays are 'arrays of arrays' in
Java.
The Arrays.sort() method uses a dual-pivot Quicksort algorithm.
ArrayList belongs to the Collections Framework and can resize dynamically.
Varargs must always be the last parameter in a method signature.

Enhanced
for-loops (for-each) are the cleanest way to iterate through arrays.

code_example_1.title: Basic 1D Array Operations
code_example_1.code:
Sorted: [Apple, Banana, Grape, Orange]
Index of Banana: 1
Center element: 5

memory_visualization:
In Java, the reference variable (e.g., 'int[] arr')
lives on the STACK. The actual array object ([10, 20, 30]) lives on the HEAP.
When you assign one array variable to another, you are copying the reference
(memory address), not the actual data. Both variables then point to the same
array in memory.
real_world_use_cases:
Storing pixels in an image (2D
arrays).
Implementing undo/redo buffers in text editors.

Managing a fixed
list of configuration settings.

Matrix math in scientific computing
applications.
common_mistakes:
Thinking the last index is the length of
the array (it is length - 1).

Trying to resize an array after creation (use
ArrayList instead).

Forgetting to initialize the array with 'new', leading to
NullPointerException.

Comparing two arrays using '==' (checks memory address)
instead of 'Arrays.equals()' (checks content).
best_practices:
Use the
enhanced for-loop unless you need the index for logic.

Prefer ArrayList if
the number of elements is unknown or dynamic.

Use Arrays.toString() for easy
debugging/printing of array contents.

Always validate the index before
accessing to prevent crashes.

faq.question:
Can an array store
different data types?
faq.answer:
No, Java arrays are homogeneous. You can only
store one type (e.g., all int, all String). However, an Object[] array can store
any object because all classes inherit from Object.

faq.question:
What is
the default value of array elements?
faq.answer:
Numeric types (int, double)
default to 0/0.0, booleans default to false, and objects/references default to
null.

summary:
Arrays provide a structured way to handle collections of
data efficiently. While fixed in size, they offer the best performance for
indexed access. Combined with the Arrays utility class and the modern ArrayList,
they form the backbone of data management in Java.
key_takeaways:
Arrays
are objects stored in the Heap.
Index starts at 0 and ends at length - 1.
The 'length' property is final and read-only.
Arrays.sort() is the standard way to order elements.
ArrayList is a wrapper that provides a dynamic-size array.
Binary search requires the array to be sorted first.
preview_of_next_topic:
In
Phase 5, we transition from data structures to the heart of Java:
Object-Oriented Programming (OOP) Basics, covering Classes, Objects, and
Constructors.

questions.question:
What happens when you try to access numbers[5] in an
array of size 5?
questions.answer:
Java will throw an
'ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException' at runtime. In a size-5 array, the valid
indices are 0, 1, 2, 3, and 4. Index 5 is outside the bounds.

questions.question:
Explain how a 2D
array is stored in memory.
questions.answer:
Java treats a 2D array as an 'array of
arrays'. The main reference points to an array in the Heap where each element is
itself a reference to another 1D array object. This allows for 'ragged arrays'
where rows can have different lengths.

questions.question:
Write a snippet to find the maximum
value in an integer array named 'data'.
questions.answer:
int max = data[0]; for(int
i=1; i<data.length; i++) { if(data[i] > max) max = data[i]; } This iterates
through the array and updates the 'max' variable whenever a larger value is
found.

questions.question:
Write a method using Varargs that calculates the sum of any number
of integers.

Find the bug: int[] arr =
new int[3]; arr[0]=1; arr[1]=2; arr[2]=3; arr[3]=4;
questions.answer:
The bug is
'arr[3]=4;'. The array is initialized with size 3, meaning valid indices
are 0, 1, and 2. Writing to index 3 throws an ArrayIndexOutOfBoundsException.

questions.question: Equal
Not Equal
questions.answer:
The output is 0.
First, Arrays.sort(vals) rearranges the array to {2, 5, 8}. Then, binarySearch
finds the value '2' at index 0.

questions.question:
Write a small implementation to reverse an
array in-place without using a second array.
questions.answer:
for(int i = 0; i <
arr.length / 2; i++) { int temp = arr[i]; arr[i] = arr[arr.length - 1 - i];
arr[arr.length - 1 - i] = temp; } This swaps elements from the ends moving
towards the middle.

mcqs.question: Which of the following is true about Java arrays?

options.A:
Arrays
can change their size dynamically.
options.D: An array can store multiple data types.

mcqs.question:
What is the default
value of an element in a newly created 'boolean' array?

What is the output? int[] x =
{1, 2, 3}; System.out.println(x.length);

What is the output? int[][] a = {{1, 2}, {3, 4, 5}};
System.out.print(a[1][2]);

What is the output? String[] s = new String[2];
System.out.print(s[0]);

options.B:
Line 2
(length is a property, not a method)

mcqs.question:
What is wrong with:
public void test(int... nums, String name) { }

options.A:
Varargs must
be the last parameter.
options.B: Varargs cannot be used with String.
options.C:
The
syntax should be int nums[].

mcqs.question:
How do you declare a dynamic list that can
grow? _______ list = new _______<>();

Which is the most efficient way
to copy an entire array to a new one?
